author: @BrendanMason-o7g
text:
Excellent video Rob.
What occurs to me is that the process of learning is a little like what goes on with a generator... you put work in and after a process, you get a useful result. If you don't put the effort in you will get a poor result, likewise, if you have an inefficient process, you are likely to get poor results.
Just a thought that you inspired.
Thanks Rob.

author: @richardphillips2405
text: I took your video to heart.  I have always had trouble learning complicated subjects quickly.  I considered myself as not being able to learn the material and so, I stayed away for it.  I finally accepted the fact that I might not understand much on a first go of it.  One thing that has helped, especially in mathematics, is to find applications where you can actually use the material.  One example is trig.  I started to really learn trig when I had to use trig in dealing AC circuit analysis.  I wish that you could do a follow up video as to what to do when you are trying to learn a complicated subject and you can't follow what is being said.  It is like running into a brick wall.  Instead of feeling discouraged or wanting to be rescued, how can a person develop a positive mind set.  Are there skills that can be learned that would help a person deal with hard and complicated subjects?  Thank you for your video.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers for the post mate - really the only advice is to keep trying and remember that failures are just learning what doesn't work - still learning...

author: @hansbraun2726
text: I am learning so much from you.  It is my nature to be lazy in my learning just as you say.  Thank you for teaching us .

author: @martinbraganca3303
text:
I started watching your videos a year ago, and it got me off of the chair wanting to investigate graphene. As im a 20 yr old student (from Uruguay), I cant build myself a lab, so I decided to approach university labs. Now, im at a lab in Brazil, finally testing some things I read, and already have some ideas to try. I have to thank you deeply for the motivation your videos have given me. 
Besides that, I wanted to ask you a doubt about your videos: the graphene ink you use as a base, is still the one produced by de exfoliation with acetone? It seems to me that you have moved forward from that, but I wanted to be sure. 
And a suggestion, I think color videos are a better option, cant distinguish the small supercap in the 1MegaF vid!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Martín Bragança I am most glad to hear your story mate - it makes the whole channel worthwhile - I have moved on a long way from acetone exfoliation mate - i can't share the exact method as it is a commercial secret developed for another company - i share what i can but i can't share everything - i am sure you understand that. I will move back to colour lol

replies.author: @martinbraganca3303
replies.text: sure, you already give us much information as it is! lol. But cant help myself to ask...is it still a blender/mixer exfoliation method? some tweaks and improvements to be sure, but not GO route right? just to avoid "wasting" too much time..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Martín Bragança it's a different method - not the go route

author: @dylynhouse5534
text: Thanks for all your posts. I think I have viewed every single one. I really feel like I'm doing the work by listening and viewing your videos. Crap sound. Crap images. Excellent information. I find myself replaying them over and over, because I can't see or hear something I think is important. I'm a neophyte to chemistry but not technology. Perhaps this channel is for those who are among the "League". For those who are not, how bout some color, sound and decent notation below. I know all three is asking a lot but how about two out of three. I'll still keep watching and experimenting and "doing the work",  whether you improve two of three or not.

author: @BluesDoctor
text: Bravo my friend. Anyone who would deny your logic would be foolish indeed, or at the least grossly self-centered ego maniac.  I am designing and building a NIR/SWIR non-imaging spectrometer if ever such access would help you. Occasionally have imaging hyperspectral NIR and less occasionally NIR/SWIR hyperspectral cameras. Have local university access to other analytical tools, just at some cost if ever a critical need develops. Particularly interested in 3D and as mentioned, packing effects. Cheers

author: @danajohnson3799
text: I have watched most of your videos and agree that you have been more than generous is showing and explaining your processes. My interest and discipline is in electronics. I have no interest in attempting to duplicate "the chemistry", I'm sure I could from what you have shown. I find your work in thin/thick film Graphite based research fascinating as it applies to electronics. I'm sure you know that major electronic component manufactures are also doing this research and have promised new super capacitor products soon. I  admire your work ethic and honesty. Thank you for teaching me so much.

author: @a0cdhd
text: Quite so Mr. Murray Smith. Quite so. Seems to be a symptom of today's 'instant gratification' society. Everything has to be 'easy' and we can blame television and media advertising for that:  fatuous, inane, vapid drivel that it is. You learn nothing without investigation. The 'what' is easy. The 'how and why' is the hard part. Buying a cake of soap (or being given one) is easy but knowing how to make soap requires investigation and the accumulation of knowledge by trial and error. The great unwashed don't seem to understand that concept.  Somebody once said, (and with due respect and recognition for the comments below: 'Give a man a fish and you'll feed him for a day, give him a fishing line and you'll feed him for life but give him too much line and he'll hang himself.'

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: a0cdhd I have this theory that humanity is at its best when it struggles

author: @cooltroops8182
text: Fair comments Robert, even when answers are given ! It's when things go wrong that you start to really learn. If you can be bothered to put the effort in !

author: @BinjKomisar11
text:
Yup... I actually said to a guy this morning when he inquired about why I was up working so early on a Sunday (as I was going at a project).. I said "The fact about all of this stuff is that it is not going to magically do itself" He replied "But can't you buy most of this stuff" then I said "Yeah some of it, but not all of it, and the reality is I wouldn't be learning anything if I never got my hands dirty"...
Then I came on youtube and saw this video... lol
I have learned so much from your videos, good info that I cherish.
Awesome stuff Mr. R M Smith, good luck on your 1MegaFarad Super Cap build!!!
Looking forward as always.
Peace
Ben

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @mixolydian2010
text: Very important video, thank you. I was at University in Huddersfield and Preston around 15 years ago. One of my lecturers said at the start, if i am remotely in doubt as to the provenance of your assignment content, i will copy and paste a paragraph etc into Google and if it comes back you will be failed. All but a few of my classmates were prepared to work with or investigate anything with anyone else, they were afraid of exposing their failings to others including the lecturer. They would say nothing in the lectures, i did and it became embarrassing for me and alienating too. You learn the theory and you experiment and test your theories. Those experiments become part of you and they physically change your perceptions and your skills in a way impossible from rote learning. Thank you very much for sharing your experiments with all of us, much appreciated, Colin

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mixolydian2010 Absolutely mate - aside from that - Huddersfield is my home town!
